content: The messenger RNA (mRNA) molecule plays a crucial role in transmitting genetic information from DNA to the ribosomes, where protein synthesis occurs. Following transcription, the mature mRNA molecule is exported from the nucleus and into the cytoplasm, where it binds to ribosomal subunits. The mRNA sequence is composed of codons, each specifying one of the 20 amino acids or a stop signal. As the ribosome reads the mRNA sequence, transfer RNA (tRNA) molecules recognize the codons and deliver the corresponding amino acids to the growing polypeptide chain. The sequence of amino acids is thus determined by the sequence of nucleotides in the mRNA, allowing the cell to produce proteins with precise primary structures. This intricate process enables the cell to translate genetic information into functional proteins, which perform a wide range of cellular tasks. The accuracy of this process is ensured by various quality control mechanisms.
